nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Pechey
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

variants.name: Peachey
meanings: Americanized form of Swiss German Bütschi (see Butsch ), via the intermediate form Bitsche. This surname is most common in PA. Compare Beachy , Beechy , and Peachy .

History: This (see 1 above) is the name of a Swiss Mennonite (Amish) family, in the US also spelled Beachy, Beachey, Beechy, and Peachy. The family is most numerous in PA and in parts of OH and IN. Its progenitor was Peter Bitsche who came to North America from Switzerland in 1767 and settled in PA. The original Swiss German form of his surname was Bütschi (it originates from Bern canton where it is still most frequent), whereas Bitsche is otherwise an Austrian surname, apparently taken to Switzerland only in recent times.

variants.name: Petchey
meanings: Norman: nickname from Old French pecch(i)et, pech(i)é (Latin peccatum) ‘fault, sin’. The name has varied freely between the Old French form (with final -(i)é spelled -(i)e, -ee, and -y) and the synonymous Middle English form pec(c)he ( see Petch ). The stressed vowel in Pecché was short, so Petchey and Petch are closest to the original pronunciation of the name, but Peachey and Peach, with a lengthened vowel, are now much commoner.

nameMeanings.name: Margaret
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: An extremely common given name from the Middle Ages onwards, derived via Old French Marguerite and Latin Margarita from Greek Margarītēs, from margaron ‘pearl’, a word ultimately of Hebrew origin. The name was always understood to mean ‘pearl’ throughout the Middle Ages. The first St Margaret was martyred at Antioch in Pisidia during the persecution instigated by the Emperor Diocletian in the early 4th century. However, there is some doubt about her name, as the same saint is venerated in the Orthodox Church as Marina . There were several other saintly bearers of the name, including St Margaret of Scotland ( d. 1093 ), wife of King Malcolm Canmore and daughter of Edmund Ironside of England. It was also the name of the wife of Henry VI of England, Margaret of Anjou ( 1430–82 ), and of Margaret Tudor ( 1489–1541 ), sister of Henry VIII , who married James IV of Scotland and ruled as regent there after his death. See also Margery , Marjorie .

variants.name: Margareta
meanings: From the Latin form of Greek Margarītēs. See Margaret .

en.lifeSummary: When Margaret de Pechey was born about 1355, in Great Thurlow, Suffolk, England, United Kingdom, her father, Simon de Pechey, was 35 and her mother, Agnes Holme, was 27. She had at least 1 daughter with John Hunt. She died about 1400, in Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 47.
